content:
@Boris Brezillon is trying to change how the assignment of address spaces to VMs work, and in doing so, he's found an issue with the way things are currently designed. The plan is to have a new type, AddressSpaceManager in charge of AS allocation.

We currently have a more rudimentary slot allocator (in slot_allocator.rs) that merely tracks which AS are free, but that doesn't reclaim slots when the VM is destroyed, nor does it keep a distinction between idle slots (which should remain in place as long as we have unassigned slots) and unassigned slots themselves. So this type is an overall improvement over slot_allocator.rs.

Ideally, we would be able to do this:

impl Drop for Vm {
   fn drop(&mut self) {
      mmu.as_manager.lock().remove_vm(self); //vm_dropped
   }
}

So the Vm must somehow store a reference to the AddressSpaceManager.

We would also need to access the VM on the fault handler, i.e.: from the Mmu threaded IRQ handler. At the moment, this means storing an Arc<Mutex<Vm>> in AddressSpaceSlotData, i.e.:

struct AddressSpaceSlotData<'a> {
    /// VM currently bound to this AS
    vm: &'a Vm, // This would be an Arc<Mutex<Vm>> instead.

    /// Last time this slot was used
    last_used_seqno: u64,
}

enum AddressSpaceSlot<'a> {
    Free,
    Active(AddressSpaceSlotData<'a>),
    Idle(AddressSpaceSlotData<'a>),
}

pub(crate) struct AddressSpaceManager<'a> {
    /// IO mem region
    iomem: Arc<Devres<IoMem>>,

    /// Number of AS slots
    slot_count: usize,

    /// AS slots
    slots: [AddressSpaceSlot<'a>; 32],

    /// Sequence number incremented each time vm_active() is called
    use_seqno: u64,
}

This creates a circular reference, because now the AddressSpaceManager holds an Arc<Mutex<Vm>>. I see two ways around this

a) rewrite it so the circular reference is gone
b) add a Weak<T> type to the kernel

I am convinced that a) is possible, but I haven't been able to come up with something so far, perhaps anyone has a better idea?

Here's a branch with an initial implementation by Boris. He would have to switch from &Vm to Arc<Mutex<Vm>>, but otherwise the basic idea is there.

cc @Alice Ryhl @Beata Michalska
You won't be able to have lifetimes on these structs, so anything involving &Vm definitely won't work.

@Alice Ryhl yeah I mentioned that

    vm: &'a Vm, // This would be an Arc<Mutex<Vm>> instead.

So I mean, what we do in Binder for this kind of thing is that there's a release method on our BinderProcess struct that runs on process exit. That method performs various cleanup which has the side-effect of breaking all cycles. I think that is the main alternative to consider.
That can work, but it's not automatic :/
i.e: given that we're trying to push safety with Rust and etc, maybe it would be better to have things on drop() as an additional safety layer

In the case of Binder, I can't really rely on the refcounts actually hitting zero for cleanup anyway, so I have to do it that way. There could be references to the objects from other binder processes.

Ah wait, we're saying slightly different things. What I mean is that there's a specific call where Vm's are destroyed (DRM_PANTHOR_IOCTL_VM_DESTROY), and that we could reach the allocator through TyrData and free the slot at that point, but that would still be a manual process.
but what you're saying is having a method to break cycles, which is slightly different
I mean, it's just a general method to perform all the cleanup tasks.
that are necessary on process exit

I wonder what impl Drop for TyrDriver does, I see it's hooked up to drm_device::release on the C side

Cc @Danilo Krummrich
It goes through each BinderThread and if there is an active transaction, then it sends a reply to notify that the process died. This also has the effect on breaking process->thread->process cycles.
Hold on. Maybe we should discuss this once I have something to show.

I wonder what impl Drop for TyrDriver does, I see it's hooked up to drm_device::release on the C side

This might be the point where we break any cycles, for example.
So it's not based on some code excerpt that are not linked to the rest of the driver
@Boris Brezillon this issue is not only about that code you've shown btw, we have the same problem with CSGs, for example. There's nothing to return CSGs to the scheduler once they're not bound.
it's a general pattern really that's been happening in a few places
As I was saying to @Daniel Almeida, I use this as an exercise to leverage some rust concepts, and at the same time, get familiar with the Tyr codebase
It might be that I'm just overthinking it
yes, CSGs have the same issue, but groups are also destroyed explicitly (with GROUP_DESTROY, or when the file is closed)

This is just me imagining things, but let's say that the vm gets unbound in three scenarios:

  1. When another vm needs it.
  2. When the vm has a fault.
  3. When the userspace that owns it closes its fd.

In that case, I think cases 2 and 3 just have to explicitly remove it.

If you lazily leave weak references around, you're also going to be consuming memory you don't need anymore. A weak reference might let you run the object's destructor, but you can't free the allocation that holds the object.

Daniel Almeida said:

I wonder what impl Drop for TyrDriver does, I see it's hooked up to drm_device::release on the C side

Cc Danilo Krummrich

TyrDriver is the driver's device private data type you return from probe(). It is dropped when the driver is unbound.

When the DRM device is dropped, it drops TyrDriver::Data which lives within the drm::Device.

The fact that you implement both traits for the same type should not have any practical relevance, other than that it can indeed be a bit confusing at a first glance. :)

I hope that helps!
@Alice Ryhl so given Danilo's answer above, even if we have a A points to B points to A using Arcs, we can still use this drop call above to undo it IIUC?
If there's a cycle, then drop doesn't run.
since it runs when the count hits zero
yeah but this drop() runs when the driver is unbound, it doesn't rely on the refcounts on each Arc
Right, yes.
Another pattern that may be useful is to have a VmMainRef(Arc<Vm>) that has a destructor for performing cleanup. This kind of thing is much more reliable than drop for an object in an Arc.
So the process has its "main" reference to the vm somewhere. And when that reference goes out of scope, it's removed.
Of course, any pattern involving drop is going to require that you take the lock in drop. A proper release method often allows you to take the lock fewer times.

So the process has its "main" reference to the vm somewhere. And when that reference goes out of scope, it's removed.

Can you expand a bit? how would the destructor here be different?

yeah but this drop() runs when the driver is unbound, it doesn't rely on the refcounts on each Arc

Right but that's not what we want.
We want VMs/Groups/xxx to go away soon after the file has been dropped, or when those objects are explicitly destroyed (xxx_DESTROY ioctls).
If you have a circular ref, like is the case with AS slots or CSGs, you need to kill this cycle explicitly

I think no matter what you need a function that runs on fd close, and in that function you should just clear CSG and VMs explicitly. RAII is nice, but it's not something that solves all your problems when you have cyclic refcounted objects.
